applicability.publicSurface: non-private typed declarations and fields visible to the pinned compiler; @:noCompletion declarations are excluded

probeScopes.reason: The real CustomTarget(c) carrier is authoritative for common and hosted C typing.

A test-only custom target exposes only the pinned upstream declarations for modules that correctly reject C until target.threaded or target.atomics has adapter evidence; it has no generator and is not a production carrier or behavior oracle for these rows.

exclusions.reason: These are implementations owned by other Haxe targets, not portable API declarations for C.
These APIs execute inside the Haxe compiler or language server and do not become generated C standard-library code.
These are JavaScript-specific HTTP implementations; the portable haxe.Http contract and sys adapter remain included.

loweringPolicy.compileTimeWork: Representation, specialization, constant/static dispatch, escape and lifetime analysis, layout and table construction, and dead-feature elimination belong in hxc whenever sound.
loweringPolicy.runtimeRule: A runtimeFeatures value is candidate ownership, never eager selection. The compiler must prove direct C and program-local specialization infeasible before requesting the smallest optimized hxrt slice.
loweringPolicy.runtimeFreeRule: An API mention alone selects nothing. A runtime-free build contains no hxrt include, source, define, library, or symbol.

diagnosticPolicies.when: A planned or partial API reaches a typed operation that has no admitted semantic lowering.
diagnosticPolicies.remediation: Use an admitted typed alternative or implement the row's ownerBeads issue with semantic and native evidence; never return a placeholder value.

diagnosticPolicies.when: An implemented API still requires a named runtime feature after direct C and local specialization, or the resolved policy forbids that feature.
diagnosticPolicies.remediation: Inspect hxc.runtime-plan.json, choose a semantics-preserving typed alternative, or select an allowed explicit runtime policy.

ownershipRules.notes: Primitive declarations and exact-width helpers remain compiler-owned and cannot select hxrt; individual admitted operations may be promoted by exact overrides.
A type token or alias alone selects no metadata runtime; reachable inspection and dynamic operations are owned separately.
These are typed compiler contracts; specialization must preserve their source meaning without wrapper allocation.
Each Std operation is specialized independently; dynamic, object, and string are only candidate fallbacks for operations that prove they need them.
Prefer constant folding, C operators, and proven C math functions; native libraries are manifested build facts, not hxrt features.
Whole-program reachability and static dispatch run first; only irreducibly dynamic metadata and graph operations may retain these slices.
ADR 0004 scalar-indexed UTF-8 applies. ASCII/direct paths and compiler-built constants precede local helpers and narrow string or Unicode slices.
Known element/key/value types should specialize to direct typed storage; allocation and tracing remain explicit reachability decisions.
Pure Haxe algorithms and iterator shapes should lower and inline through typed collection representations without an iterator runtime by default.

Byte units, bounds, partial I/O, ownership, and overflow are explicit. The admitted haxe.io.Bytes subset has exact API overrides; other binary and stream APIs remain planned, and compiler-known buffer operations should become direct C before shared I/O support.
Value-only enums, tables, and path algorithms should remain ordinary optimized Haxe-to-C code; platform filesystem meaning is owned separately.
Wall clock, local time, timezone, precision, and range require explicit adapter capabilities; freestanding has no implicit clock.
Formatting and arithmetic remain ordinary compiler-optimized Haxe once Date supplies explicit platform facts.
Single-thread and host-loop specializations run first; thread or timing support is selected only for reachable scheduling behavior.
Literal analysis and bounded specialization precede the selected documented regex backend; backend, license, and native facts remain explicit.
Proven result/status lowering is preferred; general stacks and exceptions use only the contained ADR 0006 slice and never cross native boundaries.
Compile-time generated serializers and typed fast paths should avoid reflection; dynamic parsing remains bounded and reports exact reachable features.
Pure parsing and printing stay compiler-lowered; reflection is retained only for source operations that cannot use generated typed schemas.
Compile-time position facts and static formatting belong in generated C; an actual output sink is an explicit I/O decision.
The pinned pure-Haxe algorithms should compile as optimized direct C; cryptographic suitability and side-channel claims require separate evidence.
Generic and fixed-width specialization should compile the upstream algorithms directly; transitive collection allocation remains owned by its originating operations.
The upstream fallback is intentionally incomplete on a new target; a reviewed direct C/library adapter must replace it without hiding allocation or native linkage.
The target-neutral archive algorithms should remain ordinary optimized generated C over explicit byte streams.
Arguments, environment, process, clocks, and sinks use direct libc/OS calls where valid, with explicit capability and escaping policies.
POSIX, Windows, WASI, and Emscripten facts remain adapter-owned; resource cleanup and shell-free argument handling are mandatory.
Sockets, DNS, TLS, blocking, timeout, certificate, and error semantics require an exact hosted adapter and no external-network test dependency.
The declaration-only custom-target probe does not claim C support. C must keep target.threaded and target.atomics absent until adapter, memory-model, root, and race evidence exists.

apiOverrides.notes: The nominal fixed-length shared Bytes representation, local ownership, owned returns, and exact selective runtime closure are admitted; unlisted methods and managed values inside aggregate or Array elements remain source-positioned boundaries.
Run-time non-negative lengths allocate exact shared storage and haxe.c zero-initializes it deliberately; invalid lengths and allocation failures use the checked status edge.

selector.signature: (pos : Int, src : haxe.io.Bytes, srcpos : Int, len : Int) -> Void

apiOverrides.notes: Named live source and destination values support checked overlap-safe copying; an unowned fresh nested source is rejected until expression-temporary cleanup is admitted.
Named values use the pinned implementation's byte-prefix-first lexicographic order and then length; a fresh nested argument remains an explicit temporary-lifetime boundary.

selector.signature: (pos : Int, len : Int, value : Int) -> Void

apiOverrides.notes: A checked range is filled with the low eight bits of the Haxe Int; the operation mutates every alias to the same Bytes owner.
A checked position returns an ordinary Haxe Int in the inclusive range 0 through 255; haxe.c preserves the nominal call with its target source declaration.
The immutable fixed length is read through the managed owner and is guaranteed to fit Haxe's signed Int because construction rejects larger storage.

An admitted immutable UTF-8 String view with absent or null encoding is copied exactly, including non-ASCII scalars and embedded NUL. The expression may be a literal, local, alias, or parameter; runtime-created owned Strings and explicit encodings remain unsupported.
A checked position stores the low eight bits of the Haxe Int and the mutation is visible through every alias to the same Bytes value.
A checked range creates an independent owned copy; fresh results may bind to locals or cross an owned return boundary, while unsupported nested temporary shapes fail closed.

Ordinary Haxe String.charAt uses ADR 0004 Unicode-scalar indexing and returns a borrowed UTF-8 view of the selected character, or the empty string for a negative or out-of-range index. The focused hosted suite covers ASCII, a non-BMP character, embedded NUL, empty input, generated layouts, runtime planning, strict C11, optimization, and sanitizers; unlisted String methods and environments remain explicit unsupported boundaries.

Ordinary Haxe String.charCodeAt uses ADR 0004 Unicode-scalar indexing and returns the selected scalar value. The hosted fixture covers ASCII, a non-BMP value, managed ownership, generated layouts, strict C11, optimization, and sanitizers; unlisted String methods and environments remain unsupported.

Ordinary Haxe String.fromCharCode creates one owned UTF-8 String from a valid Unicode scalar. The hosted fixture covers direct calls, StringBuf.addChar expansion, ASCII, non-ASCII, invalid scalar replacement, ownership, generated layouts, strict C11, optimization, and sanitizers.

Ordinary Haxe String.length counts Unicode scalar values, not UTF-8 bytes, under ADR 0004. The hosted fixture proves the distinction with mixed ASCII, accented, and non-BMP text plus generated-layout and sanitizer coverage.

Ordinary Haxe String.substring returns a borrowed scalar-indexed view whose backing owner remains alive with the result. The hosted fixture covers omitted, reversed, negative, and out-of-range bounds together with aggregate ownership and sanitizers.

The ordinary upstream StringBuf class is admitted for the bounded constructor, addChar, and toString path used by the UTF-8 lexer. It is not a compiler-specific replacement, and unlisted StringBuf members remain unsupported.
The upstream inline StringBuf.addChar method compiles naturally through String.fromCharCode, concatenation, and managed reassignment. The compiler does not recognize StringBuf syntax specially.

The ordinary zero-argument StringBuf constructor initializes its managed String field and receives normal generated-class cleanup. The hosted fixture proves stack construction and destruction without a special runtime StringBuf object.

The upstream inline StringBuf.toString method returns the built managed String through the compiler's normal owned-return rules. The source API stays ordinary Haxe and does not expose the runtime carrier.

Typed Std.int lowering and its defined NaN, infinity, overflow, and truncation behavior are covered by ADR 0008 differential, strict-C, optimization, and UBSan evidence for the hosted production environment; other environment adapters are not yet claimed.

Only a compiler-known valid String literal is admitted. It emits a byte-counted UTF-8 value and selects the hosted io closure; general Dynamic or runtime String values remain source-positioned HXC1001 boundaries.

Only a compiler-known String literal with the pinned compiler's exact default position object is admitted. The logical file and line prefix is folded deterministically; custom parameters, rebinding, and general Dynamic values remain unsupported.
